one_liner: A 17-year-old boy presents to the ED with 18 hours of worsening abdominal pain that began around his umbilicus and has now settled in the right lower quadrant. He has had no appetite since last night and vomited twice.

stage2.hpi_extension: Pain began as a vague periumbilical ache yesterday evening, then migrated to the right lower quadrant over several hours and is now sharp and constant. He has anorexia, nausea, and two episodes of non-bilious vomiting. He feels warm and has not passed stool today. He denies dysuria, hematuria, or diarrhea. He is sexually inactive.
stage2.social_history: High school junior. No tobacco, alcohol, or drugs.
stage2.physical_exam: Lying still on the stretcher, uncomfortable. Abdomen: maximal tenderness at McBurney point (1/3 from ASIS to umbilicus) with voluntary guarding and localized rebound tenderness. Rovsing sign positive (palpation of LLQ elicits RLQ pain). Psoas sign positive (RLQ pain on right hip extension) and obturator sign positive (RLQ pain on internal rotation of the flexed right hip). Bowel sounds diminished. No CVA tenderness.

lab_results.result: WBC 15.8 (x10^9/L) with left shift and bandemia (neutrophils 82%). Hgb and platelets normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Mild to moderate leukocytosis with left shift is typical of acute appendicitis and supports an active bacterial/inflammatory process.

lab_results.result: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast: dilated appendix (11 mm) with wall thickening, periappendiceal fat stranding, and an appendicolith. No free air or abscess.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Classic CT features of acute appendicitis. An appendicolith obstructing the lumen is the most common precipitant in adolescents and young adults.

lab_results.result: Trace leukocytes, no nitrites, no bacteria, no blood. No casts.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Essentially normal UA helps exclude UTI/pyelonephritis and ureteral stone as the cause of RLQ pain.

stage5.final_clue: He is taken to the OR for laparoscopic appendectomy. Intraoperatively the appendix is inflamed and erythematous with an obstructing fecalith; pathology confirms acute suppurative appendicitis with transmural neutrophilic infiltration.

debrief.classic_presentation: Acute appendicitis classically presents with periumbilical pain (visceral referred pain from the midgut) that migrates to the right lower quadrant (McBurney point) over 12–24 hours as inflammation irritates the parietal peritoneum. Anorexia, low-grade fever, nausea, and vomiting are common. Exam findings include Rovsing, psoas, and obturator signs and localized rebound tenderness.
debrief.pathophysiology: Obstruction of the appendiceal lumen (most commonly by a fecalith/appendicolith in adults, or lymphoid hyperplasia in children) leads to increased intraluminal pressure, venous congestion, bacterial overgrowth, mucosal ischemia, and transmural inflammation. If untreated, the appendix progresses to gangrene and perforation with localized abscess or diffuse peritonitis. The appendix is a true diverticulum at the base of the cecum and its position is variable, which explains atypical presentations.
debrief.treatment_overview: Treatment is prompt appendectomy (laparoscopic preferred) plus perioperative IV antibiotics covering gram-negative and anaerobic bowel flora (e.g., cefoxitin or ceftriaxone + metronidazole). IV fluids and NPO status preoperatively. Perforated appendicitis with abscess may be managed initially with percutaneous drainage and antibiotics followed by interval appendectomy.
high_yield_pearls: Pain migration from periumbilical to RLQ is the single most reliable historical feature; it reflects progression from visceral to parietal peritoneal pain.
McBurney point = 1/3 of the distance from the right ASIS to the umbilicus — the classic surface landmark of the appendiceal base.
Rovsing sign (LLQ palpation -> RLQ pain), psoas sign (retrocecal appendix), and obturator sign (pelvic appendix) are the high-yield eponyms.
In children the most common cause of luminal obstruction is lymphoid hyperplasia; in adults it is a fecalith. Appendiceal carcinoid should be considered in older patients.
The appendix is a midgut derivative (T10 dermatome), explaining initial periumbilical referred pain.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Gastrointestinal chapter, 'Appendicitis' section.
